etymology.description: 歹(歹) (dǎi, "skeletal remains") + 刀(刀) (dāo, "knife") -> to tear apart
etymology.interpretation: The character 列(liè, "line up") is an ideogrammic compound where the component 歹(歹) (dǎi, "skeletal remains") combined with 刀(刀) (dāo, "knife") evokes the action of dividing or tearing something apart, akin to arranging skeletal remains. This concept connects to the sense of orderly arranging or lining up elements.

examples.chinese: 在火车站，大家排成一列(在火車站，大家排成一列)。
examples.pinyin: Zài huǒchē zhàn, dàjiā páichéng yī liè.
examples.english: At the train station, everyone lined up.

examples.chinese: 学生们依次列队进入教室(學生們依次列隊進入教室)。
examples.english: The students lined up in order to enter the classroom.

examples.chinese: 他拥有优秀的领导才能(他擁有優秀的領導才能)，总是能把团队列成一条线(總是能把團隊列成一條線)。
examples.pinyin: Tā yǒngyǒu yōuxiù de lǐngdǎo cáinéng, zǒng shì néng bǎ tuánduì liè chéng yī tiáo xiàn.
examples.english: He has excellent leadership abilities and can always line up the team in a row.
